food_subgroup: Bread and breadstick products with cured meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a breadstick food product class (FOODON_03301710); no specific grissini-with-prosciutto composite class exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers plain bread sticks (grissini). The Parma ham component is not captured in this code; closest parent used for the dominant grain component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for grissini with Parma ham. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this composite food item.

serving.description: 1 serving (approx. 6 grissini with 3 slices Parma ham)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving consists of approximately 6 grissini breadsticks (about 60 g) wrapped with 3 slices of Parma ham (about 60 g), totalling approximately 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate: USDA FoodData Central — Breadsticks, plain (FDC ID 172023) and Prosciutto, dry-cured (FDC ID 167903); weighted 50:50 by serving mass
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for grissini with Parma ham as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for plain breadsticks (FDC ID 172023, SR Legacy) and dry-cured prosciutto (FDC ID 167903, SR Legacy) in a 50:50 mass ratio reflecting the described serving. Per-100g values represent the blended composite.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Bread, white wheat, plain (GI ~70 referenced for plain white-flour breadstick analogue)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grissini with Parma ham as a composite. Plain grissini are made from refined white wheat flour and have a GI analogous to white bread (~70). The Parma ham component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the composite GI. GI of 70 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white wheat bread (GI=70) as the closest defensible analogue for the breadstick component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 40.8) / 100 ≈ 28.6, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 34.0 × 1.2 = 40.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Parma ham curing process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish, as the sodium content of approximately 820 mg per 100 g (equivalent to ~984 mg per 120 g serving) represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg and may contribute to elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) from refined wheat flour breadsticks
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish with caution, as the refined wheat flour grissini carry a high GI of 70 and a glycemic load of approximately 28.6 per serving, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control and pairing with low-GI foods or fibre-rich vegetables is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from cured pork ham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of high sodium (~820 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g) from the Parma ham warrants moderation; occasional small servings may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern that is otherwise low in sodium and saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grissini with Parma Ham is a classic Italian antipasto consisting of thin, crispy baked breadsticks (grissini) originating from the Piedmont region of northern Italy, wrapped with delicate slices of Prosciutto di Parma, a dry-cured Italian ham produced in the Parma province of Emilia-Romagna under protected designation of origin (PDO) status. The grissini are made from refined wheat flour, water, olive oil, yeast, and salt, baked until golden and brittle with a light, airy crunch. The Parma ham is thinly sliced, pale pink, and mildly salty with a characteristic sweet, nutty flavour derived from the traditional air-curing process lasting a minimum of 12 months. Together, the combination offers a textural contrast between the crisp breadstick and the silky, yielding ham, making it a popular starter or aperitivo accompaniment in Italian restaurants worldwide, including in Singapore where it is served at Italian dining establishments. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~280 kcal/100 g), high in protein (~14.5 g/100 g) from the prosciutto, and moderate in carbohydrate (~34 g/100 g) from the breadstick. The sodium content is high (~820 mg/100 g) due to the curing process, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The refined wheat flour base gives the grissini a high glycemic index (~70), which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets and contains gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for grissini with Parma ham as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by blending USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for plain breadsticks and dry-cured prosciutto in a 50:50 mass ratio. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white wheat bread, GI=70) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not based on a direct measurement of this specific composite dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for plain breadsticks (FDC ID 172023) and dry-cured prosciutto (FDC ID 167903) blended at 50:50 by mass. Actual restaurant values may vary depending on breadstick recipe, ham thickness, and portion size. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not consistently reported across both component source entries in USDA FoodData Central at the required precision for a composite estimate. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for either breadsticks or prosciutto. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for these components. 5. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0 — confirmed zero; neither plain breadsticks nor dry-cured prosciutto contain meaningful vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat bread from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for grissini with Parma ham exists in approved sources. 7. The dish is not halal due to the pork-derived Parma ham. 8. Price data (SGD 27.14) is preserved from the field survey data source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. 9. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the blended USDA component data.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy breadstick and prosciutto entries (50:50 mass blend) as no single approved-source entry exists for this dish.
Adopted GI=70 from University of Sydney GI Database white wheat bread analogue with full documentation of estimation basis.
Applied match_type 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented vitamin_c_mg=0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all romanized and English alias strings appear verbatim in both structures.
Set gi_category='high' consistent with GI=70 (≥70 threshold).
Confirmed G6 multilingual compliance: Italian cuisine has no mandatory non-English language requirement; romanized Italian name provided as supplementary information.
